INDIA DATA CENTRE COOLING: $2–2.5 BN OPPORTUNITY DRIVEN BY AI & HYPERSCALE DEMAND

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• Rapid data centre expansion and AI workloads to drive $2–2.5 billion investments in cooling solutions  

KEY DRIVERS  
• Data centre capacity up ~4x since 2020 to ~1.5 GW  
• Expected to reach ~3–3.5 GW over next 5 years  
• Rising rack density and AI workloads increasing heat intensity  
• 75%+ new capacity linked to hyperscale players (Google, Meta, Microsoft)  

TECHNOLOGY SHIFT  
• Transition from traditional air cooling to advanced liquid cooling  
• Growing adoption of immersion cooling technologies  
• Focus on energy efficiency and thermal optimization solutions  

INDUSTRY LANDSCAPE  
• Global leaders: 0, 1  
• Indian startups entering niche cooling and efficiency segments  
• Large operators: 2, 3, 4 adopting advanced cooling  

CHALLENGES  
• Water usage constraints in large-scale cooling systems  
• Sustainability and energy efficiency becoming critical factors  

STRATEGIC TAKEAWAYS  
• AI-driven demand structurally reshaping data centre infrastructure  
• Cooling emerging as a high-growth ancillary segment  
• Opportunity for both global incumbents and domestic

Indian credit cards are getting worse. Fast.

Here's what changed in 2026 and what it means for your wallet 👇

1) Cashback SBI Card
Online cashback slashed from 5% to a ₹2,000/cycle cap. That's a 60% cut if you were maximising it.

2) Amex Platinum Travel
You now need to spend ₹7L to get what ₹4L used to get you. Same benefits, 75% more spend required.

3) ICICI Emeralde Private Metal
iShop reward points crashed from 18% value back → 3%. And buying Amazon Pay GVs now costs 4.13% in fees — more than your reward rate.

4) Axis Bank
Lost Qatar Airways, Marriott Bonvoy, Accor. Replaced with lower transfer-ratio partners. A quiet but painful devaluation for travel hackers.

**HDFC Infinia Metal
From FY 2026-27**: spend ₹18L/year or maintain ₹50L relationship value just to keep the card.

**The pattern is clear**: banks are making premium cards harder to justify unless you're a very high spender.
Review your card stack. The math has changed.

Nifty 50 delivered one of the weakest annual returns in the last 6 years at -5%

Whenever Nifty delivers 2 years of muted returns, the probability of stronger returns in the following year historically increases meaningfully

Nifty Midcap 100 has been one of the best performing indices over the last several years with returns remaining positive across last 6 years

Smallcaps tend to be the most volatile segment of the market, corrections are usually sharper but rebounds are also significantly stronger once liquidity improves

After the recent correction, Nifty is now trading around 17.7x, which is roughly a 15% discount to its long period average valuation of about 20.9x

India’s valuation premium versus emerging markets has now moderated to around 27% compared to a 10 year average of ~73% and a peak of ~145%.

China's controls distinguish between "primary products" (magnet alloys, metal powders, SmCo/NdFeB alloys) which NEED export licenses, and "deeply processed products" (motors, speakers, electronic components) which are NOT restricted.

Simply China is saying — "Don't buy our raw magnets. Buy our finished motors, our EVs, our electronics." This is forced downstream migration. The value capture shifts entirely to China. This is industrial policy dressed as export control.

In October 2025, China made its rules stricter by adding five more rare earths to its list. They also started demanding a special license for products made anywhere in the world, as long as they contained even a tiny amount (0.1% or more) of Chinese rare earths.

However, China paused these new October rules for one year starting in November 2025 because things calmed down a bit with the US. Even with this pause, their older rules from April are still in full effect.

Pharma Intermediates | CDMOs & APIs | Supply Chain Shift 🇮🇳

European market-share has decreased & dependency increased

Regulatory Starting Materials (RSM) - RSMs are first chemical substances compliant with GMP mainly imported from cost competitive regions (China & India) to Europe

GMP Intermediates - In recent years, the share of imported GMP intermediates has increased with the development of regulatory-compliant Asian suppliers

CDMOs & APIs - APls with simple molecules being mainly imported while patented or more complex APIs are still produced locally

INDIA OIL STRATEGY SHIFT: RUSSIAN IMPORTS SURGE AMID HORMUZ DISRUPTION

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• India increases Russian crude imports by ~90% as Strait of Hormuz disruptions impact traditional supply routes  

KEY DATA POINTS  
• Russian Oil Imports: ↑ ~90% (March spike)  
• Overall Oil Imports: ↓ ~15%  
• LNG Imports (Qatar): ↓ ~92%  
• LPG Imports: ↓ ~40%  

SUPPLY RESPONSE  
• Rapid pivot towards Russian crude supplies  
• Increased sourcing from African nations like Angola  
• Diversification across 40+ countries to ensure energy security  

DRIVING FACTORS  
• Disruptions in Strait of Hormuz (critical global oil chokepoint)  
• Supply instability from Gulf nations  
• Need to secure uninterrupted and cost-effective crude supply  

STRATEGIC SHIFT  
• Russia re-emerges as a key supplier in India’s energy mix  
• Reduced reliance on West Asia amid geopolitical uncertainty  
• Greater emphasis on diversified sourcing strategy  

STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE  
• Enhances resilience against geopolitical supply shocks  
• Strengthens India’s bargaining power in global oil markets  
• Highlights importance of multi-source procurement strategy  

WHAT TO WATCH  
• continuity of Russian supply levels amid sanctions  
• Stability in Hormuz region and Gulf supply normalization  
• Impact on refining margins and import costs  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Strong proactive response ensures supply continuity  
• Risk – Continued geopolitical tensions may keep volatility high

THE UNLUCKIEST INVESTOR: POWER OF LONG-TERM COMPOUNDING

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• Invests at 52-week high every year for 35 years  
• Still generates ~10.5% CAGR despite worst timing  

SCENARIO DETAILS  
• Strategy: Annual investment at market peak  
• Total investment: ₹35 Lakh  
• Final value: ₹3+ Crore  
• Outcome: Market falls after each investment (consistently unlucky timing)  

KEY INSIGHTS  
• Time in market > timing the market  
• Equity compounding over long periods overrides poor entry points  
• Consistency and discipline create wealth  

STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE  
• Demonstrates resilience of equity markets over long term  
• Reflects underlying earnings and economic growth of India  
• Highlights importance of staying invested  

LIMITATIONS  
• Returns could be higher with better timing  
• Drawdowns would be significant during bad entry points  
• Does not eliminate importance of valuation awareness  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Reinforces long-term investing discipline and power of compounding

MAITHAAN ALLOYS: RATING DOWNGRADED

- Short-term rating unchanged at A1+.  

Weak Financial Performance  
- 9M FY26 operating income at ₹1,613 Cr due to low volumes and realizations.  

Cost and Margin Pressures  
- Power costs ~30% of total costs, compressing margins to 7–10% (vs 13–37% earlier).  
- Power tariffs up to ₹6+/unit from ₹4–5, hitting production costs.  

Realization Decline  
- Realizations dropped to ~₹80,000/ton vs ₹1,10,000+/ton previously.  

Investor Impact  
- Negative — weak profitability and cost pressures triggered the downgrade.

IRAN HORMUZ DEAL THREATENS PETRODOLLAR  

Surface Diplomacy, Deep Financial Impact  
- Iran offered EU transit access through Strait of Hormuz.  
- This strait carries 20% of global oil supply.  

Europe's Energy Crisis Escalates  
- Europe's energy bill surged $16.2 billion in 30 days.  
- Natural gas up 100%, oil up 60%, diesel at $200/barrel.  
- Dollar reserves already down from 70% to 56.9% in 25 years.  

Petrodollar System at Risk  
- Deal would likely settle in euros, not dollars.  
- One major non-dollar oil deal proves bypass is possible.  
- Petrodollar (born 1974) forces global dollar demand for oil purchases.  

Domino Effect on US Dollar  
- Cracks accelerate BRICS momentum, Gulf states reconsider.  
- Dollar demand collapses, US unable to fund $34T debt easily.  
- ECB's Panetta (Apr 2): "Even if Iran war ends, damage is done."  

Real War: Reserve Currency Control  
- Iran blocks Hormuz for US, opens for EU deal.  
- EU, desperate, may accept euro/yuan settlement.  
- Global South, BRICS see: "If EU bypasses dollar, so can we."  
- Leads to falling dollar reserves, US inflation spike.

HDFC BANK: NO LEGAL ACTION ON CHAKRABORTY  

- ETNIW sources: HDFC Bank not pursuing legal action against ex-Chairman Atanu Chakraborty despite reputational concerns.  
- External firms Trilegal and Wadia Ghandy & Co appointed for independent review.  
- Scope covers governance processes, accountability, and internal controls.  
- Bank prioritizing internal review over litigation.  
- Board already noted issues from resignation letter.  
- Law firms to submit separate reports for potential further steps.  
- Focus on strengthening governance and board oversight.

Time Beats Timing

The ultimate proof that consistency trumps luck. This data tracks a "worst timing" investor who put ₹1 lakh into the Sensex every single year at its absolute peak (the 52-week high) from 1991 to 2025.

The result? Even by buying at every bubble—from Harshad Mehta to the Dotcom crash and the GFC—a total investment of ₹35 lakh ballooned into over ₹3.05 crore. It’s a powerful reminder that in the market, your "time in" the index is far more important than "timing" your entry. Even the worst entry points eventually turn green if you have the patience to stay invested.

**Why read The Art of Execution?**

Most investors focus on what to buy. This book focuses on what separates average investors from extraordinary ones: how they execute after the buy.

**Key Insights:**
Buying isn’t everything – Execution determines your fate.
Categorize yourself – Are you a Rabbit, Assassin, or Hunter? Your style matters.
Cut losers fast – Great investors are ruthless with their mistakes.
Double down on winners – The best add aggressively to successful positions.
Mindset beats IQ – Discipline and self-awareness matter more than predictions.

**Why read it?**
If you've ever had the right stock but still lost money, this book shows how elite investors turn even poor decisions into profits—with better execution.

WAR-HIT SECTORS SUPPORT IN WORKS  

- Finance Ministry is reportedly preparing pre-emptive relief for war-hit sectors.  
- A new credit scheme of over Rs 2 lakh crore is being planned, similar to ECLGS.  
- The move aims to cushion stress in export-linked sectors.  
- MSMEs remain the most exposed due to high interest-rate sensitivity.  
- DFS is also working on a fresh scheme, likely to be rolled out in 15 days.  
- It may offer collateral-free loans with government guarantees, especially for MSMEs.  
- Officials say there is no immediate stress, but a buffer is being built for the economy.  
- Even if the war ends, affected sectors may take time to recover.

TRUMP’S PHARMA TARIFF BLITZ  

- 100% tariff on branded & patented drugs + APIs not cutting U.S. prices or shifting production.  
- 20% tariff for firms committing to move manufacturing to the U.S.  
- 0% tariff for companies lowering prices & investing locally.  
- Generics, biosimilars excluded.  
- EU, Japan, Switzerland, Korea: 10–15% tariff; UK: ~10%.  
- Could spark global pharma realignment — India’s generics may gain big.

AVIATION SEAT CHARGE RULE: GOVT U-TURN EASES PRESSURE ON AIRLINES

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• Govt puts 60% free seat rule on hold, maintaining status quo for airlines  

TIMELINE  
• Mar 17: Ministry directs DGCA to ensure 60% free seats  
• Mar 20: DGCA issues circular (effective Apr 20, 2026)  
• Apr 2: Rule kept in abeyance until further notice  

WHAT WAS PROPOSED  
• 60% seats to be free (vs ~20% currently)  
• Seat selection fees: ₹200 – ₹2,100  

WHY GOVT ACTED  
• Rising passenger complaints on high seat charges  
• Airlines monetizing up to ~80% seats  
• Focus on passenger rights and transparency  

AIRLINE RESPONSE  
• Opposition from IndiGo, Air India, SpiceJet  
• Concern over loss of ancillary revenue  
• Risk of fare hikes amid high fuel costs  

WHAT STILL CONTINUES  
• Transparent seat allocation policies  
• Co-seating for same PNR passengers  
• Disclosure of extra charges (baggage, pets, etc.)  
• Passenger rights protections remain intact  

KEY TAKEAWAY  
• Rule is paused (not scrapped); policy uncertainty remains  
• Airlines retain flexibility on ancillary pricing  

IMPACT  
• Positive for airlines – Avoids revenue hit and preserves pricing power

In our conversation with Col MacGregor he said - it’s time for the world to move from “Just-In-Time” inventory to “Just-In-Case” 

Every node is now a pressure point.

Ideal setup for buying quality businesses:

Lower Valuations + Higher ROEs + Higher cashflows/FCF Yield + Higher Dividend Yield

All of these are in favour of IT Companies. What is not in the favour, is the narrative in the market.

Is the narrative right or wrong, only time will tell but what is in our control is the entry point. And good high quality businesses are not available at cheap valuations unless there is high pessimism against it.
The courage to go against the narrative is rare.

“RBI sets volatility margin on USDINR forwards to curb swings”?

In simple words:  

- USDINR forwards are contracts where banks/players agree to buy or sell dollars at a future date at a fixed rupee rate.  
- Volatility margin means regulators are asking traders to put up extra margin (more cash or collateral) when they trade these USDINR forward contracts.  

Why is this done?  
- To reduce wild swings in the forward rate of USDINR.  
- Bigger speculation makes the dollar–rupee rate jump around more. Extra margin makes it costlier to trade highly, so traders take smaller, more cautious bets.  
- This helps stabilise the rupee and protects banks and the system from big losses if the currency moves sharply.  

In short:  
RBI is tightening rules on USDINR forward trades by adding a volatility‑linked margin so that markets don’t move too wildly and stay more stable.

MUMBAI HOSPITAL OPPORTUNITY: LAND ECONOMICS VS EXECUTION REALITY

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• Mumbai healthcare market remains structurally undersupplied in key micro-markets  
• Recent land deals by Medanta and Jupiter highlight sharply divergent capital allocation strategies  

INDUSTRY CONTEXT  
• MMR beds/10k population: 34 (vs Delhi NCR 27, India 16)  
• Core Mumbai skew: ~75% beds concentrated in Western line  
• Kalyan-Dombivli severely underserved (~10 beds/10k population)  
• High share of trust hospitals limits aggressive capacity expansion  
• Corporate penetration still low vs Delhi NCR (Max, Fortis, Apollo, Medanta dominated)  

KEY LAND DEALS  
• Medanta:  
  – Location: Oshiwara (Andheri)  
  – Land: 2.2 acres  
  – Cost: ₹125 Cr  
  – Planned capacity: 750 beds  
  – Land cost/bed: ~₹16.7 lakh  

• Jupiter:  
  – Location: BKC  
  – Land: 2.5 acres  
  – Cost: ₹354 Cr  
  – Planned capacity: 500 beds (likely to increase)  
  – Land cost/bed: ~₹88 lakh  

CAPEX & UNIT ECONOMICS  
• Tier 1 hospital capex: ₹0.8–1 Cr/bed (ex-land)  
• Medanta total capex: ~₹1,200 Cr (~₹1.8 Cr/bed)  
• Jupiter estimated capex: ₹2.2 Cr/bed++  
• BKC land acquired at ~₹35,300/sq m (vs ₹3.9–4.8 lakh/sq m commercial rate)  

REVENUE BENCHMARKING (ARBOP)  
• Mumbai hospitals ARBOP: ₹50,000–₹65,000  
• Max Nanavati: ~₹77,000  
• Max Saket (benchmark): ₹90,000+  

STRATEGIC INSIGHTS  
• Mumbai’s entry barrier = land scarcity, not demand  
• Trust hospital dominance creates opportunity for corporates  
• Medanta strategy: disciplined land cost, scalable economics  
• Jupiter strategy: premium location bet, dependent on scale + pricing power  

RISKS & EXECUTION CHALLENGES  
• High capex/bed requires superior ARBOP and occupancy ramp-up  
• Talent acquisition and therapy mix critical for profitability  
• Jupiter’s current plan may be sub-optimal without bed expansion  
• Greenfield gestation risk higher vs brownfield expansions  

MANAGEMENT EDGE (JUPITER)  
• Strong regional expertise in MMR (Thane, Pune presence)  
• Existing EBITDA margins ~23% (competitive with Tier 1 peers)  
• Ongoing expansion in Dombivli strengthens cluster strategy  

IMPACT  
• Mixed –  
  – Medanta: Positive (disciplined capital allocation, better unit economics)  
  – Jupiter: Execution-dependent (high-risk, high-reward strategy in premium micro-market)

Japan’s Proterial Eyes India Entry in Rare Earth Magnet Push  

Proterial’s India plan  
- Proterial Ltd is eyeing India’s rare earth magnet incentive scheme.  
- It may partner with Indian firms to bid for subsidies.  

India’s goal  
- India wants to cut reliance on China for rare earths and magnets.  
- A ~$768 million scheme aims to build domestic magnet capacity.  

Global supply risk  
- China dominates rare earth mining.  
- Recent export curbs raise supply concerns.  

Demand and capacity  
- India targets ~6,000 tonnes of magnet output yearly.  
- EVs, wind turbines, and defence are key demand sectors.  

Domestic gaps  
- India has large rare earth reserves.  
- Refining and processing capacity is still limited.  

Policy and players  
- Policy favours global tech ties plus local manufacturing.  
- Vedanta Group, Sona BLW, and others are also eyeing the space.  

Why it matters  
- Proterial’s entry can boost India’s supply‑chain security.  
- It can cut import dependence and lift high‑value magnet manufacturing.

Hero FinCorp Aims to Double Customer Base in 3–4 Years  

Growth ambitions  
- Abhimanyu Munjal targets doubling the customer base from ~1.25 crore to 2–2.5 crore over 3–4 years.  
- Growth is being driven by deeper customer relationships and tie‑ups with Maruti, MG Motor, Spinny, and Cars24.  

Business and AUM  
- Current AUM at ~₹56,000 crore, with steady expansion even amid global and geopolitical volatility.  
- West Asia tensions have had no major impact so far; operations remain stable and on track.  

IPO and stress watch  
- IPO plans (target raise of ₹3,500 crore) are on hold due to uncertain market conditions.  
- Management is monitoring pockets of stress in chemicals, fertilisers, ceramics, and MSMEs.  

Lending mix and risk control  
- Diversified lending model allows shifting capital between retail and MSME/structured finance based on demand.  
- About 65% of the incremental book is secured, with a strong focus on maintaining asset quality.  

Digital and AI edge  
- AI handles ~60% of customer service and has cut onboarding time from 90 minutes to about 3 minutes.  
- AI‑driven efficiency is lowering costs and improving scalability without heavy branch‑level expansion.  

Overall strategy  
- The company is focused on long‑term growth rather than short‑term cycles.  
- Expect RBI’s rate stance to remain cautious amid current volatility, which the company is factoring into its planning.

INDIA POWER TRANSMISSION: CAPEX NEED & EXECUTION CHALLENGES

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
- Massive ₹9.16 lakh Cr transmission capex planned, but execution delays creating supply-demand risks  

CAPEX & TARGETS  
- National Electricity Plan (NEP 2022–32) outlines large-scale grid expansion  
- Significant investment required to support future power demand and RE integration  

EXECUTION BOTTLENECKS  
- Land acquisition and right-of-way (RoW) challenges  
- Delays in forest clearances and regulatory approvals  
- TBCB projects facing 3–18 months delays  
- 76 projects (~₹93,000 Cr) delayed as of January  

PERFORMANCE GAP  
- FY25 transmission additions at 8,830 circuit-km  
- ~42% below target of 15,253 circuit-km  
- Widening gap between planned vs executed capacity  

DEMAND OUTLOOK  
- Power demand projected to reach ~458 GW by FY32 (vs ~275 GW currently)  
- Growth driven by rising electricity consumption and renewable energy expansion  

STRATEGIC IMPACT  
- Delay in transmission infrastructure can constrain power evacuation  
- Risk of bottlenecks in renewable energy integration  

KEY INSIGHT  
- Execution remains the biggest constraint despite strong policy push and capex visibility  

OUTLOOK  
- Acceleration in approvals, land acquisition, and project execution critical to meet future demand  

IMPACT  
- Neutral to Positive — long-term opportunity intact, but near-term execution risks remain

INDIAN BANKING SECTOR: RISING LENDING RATES IN SMALL PRIVATE BANKS

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
- Smaller private banks raising lending rates due to rising funding costs and deposit pressures  

RATE MOVEMENT  
- One-month MCLR increased by 5–10 bps in March  
- Reflects tighter liquidity and higher incremental cost of funds  

FUNDING PRESSURE  
- Weak deposit growth and elevated CD ratios  
- Increased reliance on bulk deposits and certificates of deposit (CDs)  
- CD issuances surged to ~₹2 lakh Cr in March  

BANK-WISE TREND  
- Banks witnessing MCLR hikes: Bandhan Bank, CSB Bank, IDFC First Bank, RBL Bank, Yes Bank  
- Axis Bank MCLR up ~10 bps since January  
- Larger banks like HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, and Kotak Mahindra Bank remain stable or easing  

STRUCTURAL DIVERGENCE  
- Smaller banks facing higher funding costs due to weaker deposit franchises  
- Larger banks benefiting from strong retail deposit base  

KEY INSIGHT  
- Rising MCLR driven by dependence on wholesale funding and higher incremental cost of funds  

OUTLOOK  
- Trend may persist unless deposit growth improves or system liquidity eases  

IMPACT  
- Neutral to Negative — margin pressure for smaller banks; competitive advantage for larger banks
NBC NEWS ON US INTELLIGENCE ASSESSMENTS: THE IRANIAN REGIME SHOWS NO SIGNS OF COLLAPSE

Strong Order Pipeline Boosts Outlook for Indian Shipbuilders  

Why the sector is in focus  
- Indian Navy accounts for ~21% of defence spending, with a large pending order book.  
- Key names: Mazagon Dock, GRSE, and Cochin Shipyard stand out as primary beneficiaries.  

GRSE near‑term visibility  
- GRSE FY26 revenue at ~₹6,400 crore, supported by corvettes and export vessels.  
- Healthy pipeline of defence orders enhances earnings visibility into FY27–FY28.  

Large defence opportunity pool  
- Submarines, frigates, destroyers, and landing docks together represent a ~₹3 trillion order pool.  
- Potential second Indigenous Aircraft Carrier order (~₹45,000 crore) is a key upside trigger.  

Cochin Shipyard and commercial growth  
- CSL order book pushed to ~₹23,000 crore, boosted by a big LNG ship order from CMA CGM.  
- Additional opportunities in VLGCs, survey vessels, offshore platforms, and green ships.  

Growth and execution outlook  
- Revenues expected to accelerate in FY27–FY28 as large defence and commercial orders go into execution.  
- Companies are ramping up capex, R&D, and global partnerships to bid for and deliver complex projects.  

Risks to watch  
- High client concentration on the Indian Navy.  
- Long procurement cycles and global supply‑chain dependencies remain key execution risks.

**INDIA MARKET STRATEGY: NOMURA VIEW**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
- Downgrade to Neutral; energy risks emerging as key overhang  

**RATING CHANGE**  
- India downgraded from Overweight → Neutral  

**KEY CONCERNS**  
- Risk of prolonged energy disruption  
- Elevated crude prices due to Hormuz tensions  

**MACRO IMPACT**  
- India highly vulnerable to high energy costs  
- Rising commodity prices pose earnings risk  

**MARKET RISKS**  
- MSCI India exposed to energy-sensitive sectors  
- Valuations may face pressure  

**FLOW DYNAMICS**  
- Domestic flows could slow, reducing market support  

**ADDITIONAL RISK**  
- AI disruption adds medium-term uncertainty  

**OUTLOOK**  
- Tail risks easing, but core risks remain elevated

Tonight's situation is incredibly puzzling.

In President Trump's address to the nation just now, he effectively reread many of his recent social media posts out loud.

Between threatening Iran's power plants, saying the Iran War would last 2-3 more weeks, and calling out NATO, there was nothing new.

Yet, the market is now trading like the Iran War is ramping up for another month-long escalation.

Why? Because he didn't explicitly de-escalate. 

Ironically, President Trump's address to the nation just now has imposed more pressure on the US through the market's reaction.

The market, which was finally beginning to show some signs of calming, is now highly agitated, with US oil prices back to $104/barrel, stocks down sharply, and the bond market melting down again.

Ironically, President Trump is now back to solving the problem he fixed earlier this week:

How will he contain the market?

**MACRO CONTEXT DIFFERENCE**  
- Current headwinds: geopolitical tensions, high crude, fiscal stress, weaker earnings cycle  
- Same valuation levels ≠ same opportunity  

**BOTTOM-UP REALITY**  
- Implied growth: 16.0% → 15.7% (almost unchanged)  
- Market fall absorbed by earnings downgrades, not valuation reset  

**EARNINGS TREND**  
- EBITDA CAGR (FY25–FY27): 35% → 19% (sharp cut)  
- 9/14 sectors saw FY27 FCF estimates downgraded  

**MARKET STRUCTURE**  
- Polarisation: stocks clustered in extremes (overvalued vs undervalued)  
- Weak middle ground (balanced growth vs valuation)  

**SECTORS – ATTRACTIVE**  
- IT: Implied growth ~6% (low), EBITDA ~11% → undervalued vs fundamentals  
- Telecom: Implied growth ~3.9% → lowest levels, stable outlook  
- Energy: Implied growth ~8% → cheap; risk is geopolitical  
- Power: Implied growth ~9% → attractive over 3–4 years  

**SECTORS – CAUTION**  
- Consumer Discretionary: Appears corrected but earnings cut deeper → still expensive  
- Staples: ~17% implied growth → not cheap  
- Cement: ~19% implied growth → above historical range  
- Consumer Tech: 40%+ implied growth → still priced for perfection  

**KEY FRAMEWORK (REVERSE DCF SIGNAL)**  
- High implied growth vs sector → ~62% underperformance probability  
- Low implied growth vs sector → ~51% outperformance probability  

**FLOWS IMPACT**  
- FII outflows causing sharper correction in high foreign-owned stocks  
- Short-term distortion; long-term valuation signals remain relevant  

**PORTFOLIO STRATEGY**  
- Overweight: Financials, IT, Telecom, Power  
- Selective exposure: Energy (with geopolitical caution)  
- Avoid broad buying in discretionary & staples  
- Focus on stock-specific opportunities  

**OUTLOOK**  
- Market is not broadly cheap — selective opportunities exist  
- Transition to stock-picker’s market  

**IMPACT**  
- Passive “buy the dip” strategy less effective  
- Alpha generation depends on valuation discipline & sector selection

The Strait of Hormuz is more than an oil route; it's a global lifeline that, when blocked, triggers a domino effect across every aspect of modern life.

Food Scarcity: It controls nearly half the world’s fertilizer exports. Blockages mean higher prices and lower crop yields, leading to global hunger.

Medical & Tech Crisis: We lose the by-products needed for MRI machines, semiconductors, and essential packaging.

The Green Irony: Even building wind turbines and electric cars requires the specific petroleum by-products trapped in the Gulf.

The Bottom Line: We aren't just facing a fuel shortage; we are facing a shortage of the "invisible" ingredients that keep the world fed, healthy, and moving.

🚨 FROM ORDERS TO OPPORTUNITY: CRYOGENIC OGS ENTERS BIG LEAGUE

• EIL approval unlocked → entry into PSU tenders & larger project pipeline

• Oil & Gas + LNG infra play riding global energy demand & war-driven capex

• ₹5.55 Cr LOI + ₹2.28 Cr order from Emerson

• International validation via Honeywell order (~₹1.9 Cr)

• Promoters accumulating: ~1.08 lakh shares bought in Feb–Mar

• Buying at ₹159–₹163 hints strong internal confidence

• Small cap → scaling into serious infra opportunity 🚀

**Global LNG | Morgan Stanley View**  

- ~20% of global LNG supply remains offline due to disruptions.  
- JKM prices ~85% above pre-conflict levels.  
- Australian outages worsening supply tightness.  
- ~15 mt supply shortfall expected in 2026.  
- Asia LNG demand down ~7% YoY; India down ~15%.  
- Prices likely elevated even post-de-escalation.  
- Inventory rebuilding adds upside price risk.  
- Impact: Positive for producers; Negative for consumers.

**BEL | Morgan Stanley View**  

- Maintains Overweight rating with ₹508 target price.  
- Expects ₹300 Bn order inflows in FY26 vs ₹270 Bn guidance.  
- Q4 inflows: ₹108 Bn, up 11% YoY.  
- Strong pipeline across radars, avionics, and communication systems.  
- Order book: ₹2.54 Tn vs ₹1.89 Tn YoY.  
- Execution visibility remains robust.  
- Impact: Positive.

**KEY RISKS**

**TARGET PRICE CHANGES**

**CLSA ON AIRTEL: KEY TAKEAWAYS**

**RATING & TARGET**

**KEY STRATEGIC MOVE**

**INVESTMENT VIEW**

**NOMURA ON PETRONET LNG: STRUCTURAL GAS DEMAND STORY INTACT, ATTRACTIVE VALUATION**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
• Maintains BUY with Target Price of ₹340  
• Strong long-term demand tailwinds from policy push towards gas  

**KEY CONTRACT DEVELOPMENTS**  
• Yet to sign SP&As with promoters (GAIL, Indian Oil, BPCL)  
• Expected terms include ~3% annual escalation  
• No tariff cut assumed in base case scenario  

**BUSINESS ECONOMICS**  
• Regasification cost ~5% of total end-user cost  
• Low share in value chain reduces demand sensitivity to tariff changes  
• Stable and predictable earnings profile  

**DEMAND DRIVERS**  
• Government push to shift from LPG to natural gas  
• Promotion of PNG for domestic cooking  
• Expansion of pipeline infrastructure  
• Increased allocation of domestic gas towards households  

**INDUSTRY OUTLOOK**  
• Gas consumption expected to rise structurally in India  
• Policy support likely to accelerate adoption  
• Infrastructure build-out remains key enabler  

**VALUATION COMFORT**  
• Stock trading at or below replacement cost of new LNG terminals  
• Downside appears limited from current levels  
• Attractive risk-reward for long-term investors  

**RISKS**  
• Delay in signing SP&As with promoter companies  
• Slower-than-expected gas demand growth  
• Policy or regulatory changes  
• Global LNG price volatility  

**MARKET STRATEGY (AAJ / KAL)**  
• Petronet LNG: Positive bias (valuation + demand tailwinds)  
• Gas value chain: Structural growth theme intact  
• Monitor contract finalization and volume growth  

**KEY WATCHPOINTS**  
• Signing of SP&As with promoters  
• Progress in pipeline infrastructure  
• PNG adoption trends  
• LNG price movement globally  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive – Strong policy support and attractive valuation underpin long-term growth visibility

**MORGAN STANLEY ON CREDIT GROWTH: BROAD-BASED ACCELERATION ACROSS SEGMENTS**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
• Non-food credit growth strengthened to 14.8% YoY (as of Feb 26)  
• Broad-based pickup across retail, services, industry, and agri  

**SEGMENT-WISE TRENDS**  
• Retail: Growth accelerated to 19.6% vs 18.8% MoM  
• Services: Strong growth led by NBFC lending  
• Industry: Driven by large corporates’ credit demand  
• Agri & Allied: Improved to 12.3% vs 11.4% in Jan  

**RETAIL & CONSUMER CREDIT**  
• Home loan growth stable at ~11%  
• Retail credit continues strong momentum  
• Gold loans remain elevated at 128% (vs 129% in Jan)  

**MSME & NBFC TRENDS**  
• MSME credit growth strong at 27.5% (vs 28.5% in Jan)  
• NBFC credit growth accelerated sharply to 20.9% (vs 17.9% in Jan)  
• Indicates continued credit demand from informal & semi-formal sectors  

**KEY DRIVERS**  
• Strong consumption demand  
• NBFC-led credit expansion  
• Large industry capex and working capital needs  
• Rural credit demand improving  

**TREND ANALYSIS**  
• Credit growth remains well above nominal GDP  
• Shift visible toward unsecured & high-yield segments  
• Diversified growth reduces concentration risk  

**RISKS**  
• Asset quality concerns in unsecured segments  
• Overheating in gold loans & MSME lending  
• Interest rate sensitivity impacting demand  
• Regulatory tightening risk on NBFCs  

**MARKET STRATEGY (AAJ / KAL)**  
• Banks: Positive bias (credit growth supportive)  
• NBFCs: Strong momentum but monitor risk build-up  
• PSU Banks: Likely beneficiaries of corporate lending  
• Retail-focused lenders: Watch asset quality trends  

**KEY WATCHPOINTS**  
• Sustainability of >14% credit growth  
• Asset quality trends in retail/MSME  
• RBI regulatory stance on NBFCs  
• Interest rate trajectory  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive – Strong and broad-based credit growth supports economic momentum, though risk monitoring remains critical

**MACQUARIE ON INDIA EV: ADOPTION RISING, NEW LAUNCHES DRIVING MOMENTUM**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
• BEV penetration steadily increasing in passenger vehicles  
• Premium segment expansion + new launches accelerating adoption  

**BEV PENETRATION TREND**  
• PV BEV penetration: 2.4% (CY24) → 4% (CY25)  
• Further improved to ~4–5% in Q1 CY26  
• Indicates gradual but consistent EV adoption  

**SEGMENT SHIFT (₹20–30 LAKH BAND)**  
• Share increased from 9% (CY24) → 24% (CY25) → 36% (Q1 CY26)  
• Premium EV segment seeing strong traction  
• Driven by better product offerings & features  

**KEY DRIVERS**  
• Multiple new launches in ₹20–30 lakh segment  
• 3 models from M&M  
• 1 model each from Tata Motors, Hyundai, and Maruti  
• Bigger battery packs addressing range anxiety concerns  

**INDUSTRY CHALLENGES**  
• Charging infrastructure ramp-up remains a key bottleneck  
• Limited EV-specific models; reliance on ICE-converted platforms  
• Availability constraints impacting adoption pace  

**OUTLOOK (12–24 MONTHS)**  
• BEV adoption expected to gradually rise  
• Improved product mix + infrastructure development key triggers  

**COMPANY VIEW**  
• M&M: Best positioned among PV peers in BEV space  
• Strong pipeline of EV launches supports growth visibility  
• Target Price: ₹4,168  

**2W EV SEGMENT**  
• TVS Motor strengthening EV leadership  
• Recent launch in sub-₹1 lakh segment expands accessibility  

**RISKS**  
• Weak demand environment  
• Commodity price-led margin pressure  
• Intensifying competition in EV space  

**MARKET STRATEGY (AAJ / KAL)**  
• Auto EV players: Positive bias (especially M&M, TVS)  
• Watch execution of new EV launches  
• Infra development remains key monitorable  

**KEY WATCHPOINTS**  
• EV charging infrastructure expansion  
• New EV model pipeline & acceptance  
• Battery cost trends  
• Competitive intensity  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive – Structural EV adoption trend intact, led by premium segment expansion and new launches

**JEFFERIES ON CEMENT: COST PRESSURE BUILDS, PRICE HIKES LIKELY AHEAD**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
• Cement prices largely stable MoM in March  
• Modest improvement of +1.5% QoQ in Q4FY26  
• Strong volume growth trend continues  

**DEMAND & VOLUME TRENDS**  
• Q4FY26 volume growth remains healthy  
• Builds on ~9% YoY growth seen in 3QFY26  
• Demand environment supportive across regions  

**COST PRESSURES**  
• Cost per tonne increased sharply by ~₹300/Tn  
• International coal prices ↑ 18% QoQ  
• US petcoke prices ↑ 12% QoQ  
• Packing costs surged 40–50% due to material shortages  

**PRICING DYNAMICS**  
• Industry needs ~5–7% price hike (₹20–25 per bag)  
• Required to offset rising input costs  
• Dealers indicate attempts of ₹15–50 per bag hikes in April  

**INDUSTRY ACTION SIGNALS**  
• Price hikes expected in April–May period  
• Industry pushing aggressively to pass on cost inflation  
• Success of hikes remains key monitorable  

**MARGIN OUTLOOK**  
• Margins under pressure due to cost spike  
• Recovery dependent on successful price hikes  
• Near-term profitability remains sensitive to input costs  

**MARKET STRATEGY (AAJ / KAL)**  
• Cement stocks: Watch for price hike execution  
• Cost inflation remains key overhang  
• Volume growth supportive but margins at risk  

**KEY WATCHPOINTS**  
• Success of April–May price hikes  
• Trend in coal & petcoke prices  
• Packing material cost normalization  
• Demand momentum sustainability  

**IMPACT**  
• Neutral to Slightly Negative – Strong demand but rising costs may pressure margins unless price hikes sustain

🚨 India’s $4 Billion MRO Opportunity: From Dependency to Strategic Control

India has long relied on foreign countries for maintaining its defence platforms — with 80–90% of MRO work done overseas.

That is now changing.

📌 What is MRO & Why It Matters?
MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) ensures operational readiness of aircraft, ships, and submarines.

⚠️ Without domestic MRO:
– Delays in repairs
– High costs
– Strategic vulnerability in conflict

📊 Massive Demand Build-Up

India’s fleet expansion is driving demand:

✈️ IAF: ~1,800 aircraft by 2030
🚢 Navy: 200 warships, submarines doubling
🛫 Civil aviation: ~1,500 aircraft

📈 MRO market:
– $1.2B (2025) → $4B by 2030
– 27% CAGR
– Defence = 60% of growth

💰 Policy Push (Budget 2026)

₹5,000 Cr dedicated MRO package:

✔️ 100% tax holiday (5 yrs)
✔️ GST exemptions on spares
✔️ 150% depreciation on capex
✔️ 60% localization mandate in defence MRO
✔️ ₹12,000 Cr+ FDI commitments (GE, Safran, Honeywell)

🏗️ Infrastructure is Now Real

🔹 Cochin Shipyard mega dock operational
🔹 Mazagon Dock & Hindustan Shipyard expanding
🔹 Engine MRO by Rolls Royce, GE, Tata JV
🔹 HAL composite repair capabilities

👉 India is building full-stack MRO infra across air & naval segments

🌍 Big Strategic Shifts (2026)

✔️ Karwar submarine test facility (global exclusivity)
✔️ Vietnam submarine refit deal → entry into $2B regional market
✔️ Indo-Pacific Refit Alliance (QUAD-linked opportunity)

⚙️ Economics Are Attractive

💸 ₹15,000 Cr annual forex savings potential
💰 Engine overhaul: ₹50 Cr per visit
🚢 Carrier refit: ₹1,000 Cr

📉 AI + digital twins → 30% lifecycle cost reduction

🧩 Challenges Ahead

⚠️ OEM certifications (especially submarines)
⚠️ Skilled workforce gap (1.2M needed by 2030)
⚠️ Fragmented state-level ecosystem
⚠️ Trust-building for classified defence work

📌 Final Insight

India has moved from intent → execution:

✔️ Policy ready
✔️ Infra operational
✔️ FDI committed
✔️ First export contracts signed

👉 The real test now:
Can India build trust and become the Indo-Pacific’s MRO hub?

reports.text: Nomura Report on India Oil & Gas- Excise duty cut and windfall tax to benefit OMCs

🚨 FROM ORDERS TO OPPORTUNITY: CRYOGENIC OGS ENTERS BIG LEAGUE

• EIL approval unlocked → entry into PSU tenders & larger project pipeline

• Oil & Gas + LNG infra play riding global energy demand & war-driven capex

• Order inflow momentum strong: ~₹8.1 Cr + $231K secured

• ₹5.55 Cr LOI + ₹2.28 Cr order from Emerson

• International validation via Honeywell order (~₹1.9 Cr)

• Promoters accumulating: ~1.08 lakh shares bought in Feb–Mar

• Buying at ₹159–₹163 hints strong internal confidence

• Small cap → scaling into serious infra opportunity 🚀

**VIKRAM SOLAR: BACKWARD INTEGRATION LED SOLAR PLAY WITH CYCLICAL CAPEX → FCF INFLECTION**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Transition: Module player → Integrated module + cell manufacturer  
• Capacity: Modules 9.5GW → 15.5GW; Cells 12GW (from FY27)  
• Earnings CAGR: Revenue/EBITDA/PAT ~68%/81%/110% (FY25–28)  
• Margin rebuild post FY27 via DCR mix + in-house cells  

**GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Strong solar demand: ~200GW additions (FY25–30)  
• Policy tailwinds: BCD, ALMM, DCR, PLI push localisation  
• DCR mix: ~36% volume → ~47% revenue (FY28)  
• Module volumes: 3.2GW → 9.3GW (FY26–28 ramp-up)  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE**  
• Backward integration into cells improves realisations  
• Positioned in DCR (domestic content) premium segment  
• Beneficiary of India’s localisation push vs China dependence  

**FINANCIAL PROFILE**  
• Revenue: ₹34bn → ₹162bn (FY25–28E)  
• EBITDA: ₹4.9bn → ₹29.3bn  
• PAT: ₹1.4bn → ₹13bn  
• RoE: ~16.6% → ~29% | RoIC: ~14% → ~19%  
• EBITDA/Wp: ₹2.8 → ₹1.9 → ₹3.2 (dip then recovery)  

**CAPEX & FCF INFLECTION**  
• Heavy capex: ~₹78bn (FY26–28), peak in FY27  
• FCF negative during expansion phase  
• Strong FCF generation expected from FY28 onward  

**VALUATION**  
• TP: ₹290 (~52% upside)  
• Valuation: 8x FY28E EPS (~50% discount to peers)  
• Discount factors: Late cell entry + lower integration + execution risk  
• Re-rating dependent on execution & margin delivery  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Execution risk in 12GW cell commissioning  
• Module oversupply → pricing pressure  
• Input volatility (poly, glass, silver, aluminium)  
• Policy changes (BCD/ALMM/DCR)  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Growth, Execution-Heavy) – Strong earnings inflection story, but success hinges on timely integration and margin recovery

**SHRIRAM FINANCE: HIGH-YIELD NBFC WITH ROA EXPANSION STORY**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Diversified, secured retail NBFC with strong used-vehicle & MSME franchise  
• AUM CAGR ~18% (FY26–28E) with PV, MSME & gold as key drivers  
• RoA expansion: ~3.1% → ~3.5–3.8% led by NIM improvement  
• Liability transformation post MUFG → structural CoF reduction  

**GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• AUM: ₹3.0 tn → ₹4.2 tn  
• PV & MSME: ~20–25% CAGR  
• Gold loans scaling rapidly (strong rural demand)  
• New vehicle share rising (cross-sell + lifecycle play)  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE**  
• Leadership in used vehicle financing (~97% used assets)  
• Strong rural/semi-urban underwriting franchise  
• Secured granular book (~90%+)  
• Deep OEM/customer ecosystem  

**FINANCIAL PROFILE**  
• Yield: ~16.5%+ | NIM: ~9–9.7%  
• RoA: ~3.5–3.8% | RoE: ~14%  
• Credit cost: trending <2%  
• Strong provisioning buffer (ECL ~5.9%)  

**VALUATION**  
• TP: ₹1,150 (~27% upside)  
• P/E: 17.5x → 13.3x (FY26–28E)  
• P/B: 2.7x → 1.7x  
• Re-rating driven by RoA expansion + lower CoF  

**KEY RISKS**  
• CV/MSME cycle slowdown  
• Crude/geopolitical shocks impacting borrowers  
• MSME/CE credit cost volatility  
• Rising competition in used vehicle financing  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive – Strong RoA expansion with cyclical risk exposure

**MSUMI: OPERATING LEVERAGE + CONTENT GROWTH + EV OPTIONALITY STORY**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Business model: High operating leverage + rising content per vehicle  
• Revenue CAGR: ~13% (FY26–28) driven by PV, 2W and EV growth  
• Earnings CAGR: ~27% with strong margin expansion  
• Greenfield ramp-up: Key driver of profitability and RoCE improvement  

**KEY GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Greenfield utilization: ~45% → 65–70% (FY27) → further upside in FY28  
• Content growth:  
  – PV: ~1.7x increase (EV + premiumisation)  
  – 2W: ~3x increase with EV transition  
• Segment growth: PV & 2W outperform CV and agri/off-road  
• Strong OEM pipeline visibility due to dominant market position  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE (MOAT)**  
• Market leadership: >55% PV harness share, >25% in 2W  
• High customer stickiness with OEM integration  
• Sumitomo partnership → access to advanced systems (ECUs, junction boxes, EV solutions)  
• 30 manufacturing facilities with fungible capacity (flexibility across programs)  

**VALUE CREATION MODEL**  
• Operating leverage from utilization ramp-up  
• Content per vehicle expansion → structural revenue growth  
• Mix improvement (premium vehicles + EVs) boosts margins  
• Automation + disciplined capex enhances RoIC  

**OPTIONALITY (HIDDEN VALUE)**  
• EV transition: Multi-year structural content increase  
• Premiumisation (shift to UVs): ~1.5x content uplift in PVs  
• Advanced electronics integration → move up value chain (beyond wiring harness)  

**FINANCIAL TRAJECTORY**  
• Revenue: ₹111.7bn → ₹143.0bn (FY26–28)  
• EBITDA: ₹10.6bn → ₹16.1bn (~20–26% growth)  
• EBITDA margin: 9.5% → 11.3%  
• PAT: ₹6.1bn → ₹9.9bn  
• EPS growth: 23.7% (FY27E), 31.0% (FY28E)  
• RoE: 41–50% | RoCE: 49–60% (best-in-class returns)  

**BUSINESS MIX & OPERATING LEVERAGE**  
• Revenue mix:  
  – PV ~62% (core driver)  
  – 2W ~13–14% (fastest growth)  
  – CV ~9–10%, Agri ~6%, Others ~8–9%  
• Key plants: Navagam (Gujarat), Pune, Kharkhoda → utilization ramp critical  
• Capex: ~₹2bn annually with strong FCF (₹5.1bn → ₹9.3bn)  
• High payout ratio (~85%) supported by strong cash flows  

**COST STRUCTURE & RISKS (INPUT SIDE)**  
• Gross margin stable: ~33–34%  
• Cost mix: Employee ~17–18%, Other expenses ~6–7%  
• Copper exposure: ~10% of revenue  
  – Pass-through: 60–70% (3 months), balance (6 months lag)  
• Imports: ~27–28% of RM (limited near-term disruption risk)  

**VALUATION**  
• P/E: 41x / 33x / 25x (FY26E / FY27E / FY28E)  
• EV/EBITDA: 23.9x → 15.6x (compression with growth)  
• Target Price: ₹60 (40x FY28E EPS)  
• Premium justified by:  
  – High visibility growth  
  – Strong RoE/RoCE  
  – Structural EV tailwinds  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Weak domestic auto demand  
• Competitive intensity (global players like Aptiv, Yazaki)  
• Commodity volatility (copper) with lagged pass-through  
• Currency fluctuations impacting imports  

**WHERE ASSUMPTIONS ARE AGGRESSIVE**  
• Sustained double-digit volume growth across segments  
• Smooth Greenfield ramp without delays  
• Margin expansion despite RM volatility  
• Continued premium valuation multiples  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Not a commoditized harness player → evolving into system solutions provider  
• EV + premiumisation = structural, not cyclical, growth  
• High operating leverage + content growth creates strong earnings compounding  

**WHAT TO TRACK (CRITICAL)**  
• Greenfield utilization trajectory (45% → 70%+)  
• EBITDA margin expansion vs guidance  
• EV penetration and content per vehicle trend  
• Copper price movement and pass-through efficiency  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Quality Compounder) – Strong structural tailwinds, high return ratios, but valuation leaves limited margin for error

**LLOYDS METALS: MINING → STEEL INTEGRATION STORY WITH HIGH OPERATING LEVERAGE**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Transition: Merchant miner → Integrated steel player  
• Revenue: ₹67K Cr → ₹224K Cr (FY25–FY28, ~3.3x growth)  
• EBITDA: ₹17K Cr → ₹78K Cr (massive operating leverage)  
• Margin expansion: ~29% → ~35% (mix + cost advantage)  

**KEY GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Surjagarh mine ramp-up: 10 → 26 MTPA (core engine)  
• Pellet capacity: 4 → 12 MTPA (value addition)  
• Wire rod mill (FY27): 1.2 MTPA high-margin steel  
• Future HRC plant (FY29): next growth leg  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE (MOAT)**  
• Captive iron ore cost: ~₹1,933/t vs ₹5,500–6,000/t market  
• Logistics edge: slurry pipeline (₹850–1,250/t savings)  
• Captive power → energy stability  
• Integrated model → higher EBITDA/t  

**VALUE CREATION MODEL**  
• Ore → Pellets → Steel = margin ladder  
• EBITDA/t expands significantly across value chain  
• Mix improvement (not just price cycle) drives earnings  

**OPTIONALITY (HIDDEN VALUE)**  
• Thriveni (79.8% stake):  
  – MDO business → annuity-like cash flows  
  – Value contribution: ~₹209/share  
• Copper JV (DRC):  
  – Not valued in SOTP (pure upside optionality)  

**FINANCIAL TRAJECTORY**  
• Revenue CAGR: ~33% (FY24–29)  
• EBITDA margin: ~26% → ~35%  
• PAT: ₹12K Cr → ₹64K Cr  
• ROE moderates but remains strong (~27–35%)  
• Net debt peak manageable (~1x EBITDA target)  

**VALUATION (SOTP)**  
• Standalone: ₹1,522/sh (12x FY28 EV/EBITDA)  
• Thriveni: ₹209/sh  
• Total TP: ₹1,730 (≈32% upside)  
• Valuation compresses sharply with earnings growth  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Single-asset dependency (Surjagarh mine)  
• Execution risk (multiple projects simultaneously)  
• High capex cycle → balance sheet stress  
• Commodity cycle (steel/pellet spreads)  
• Regulatory & ESG risks (mining + DRC exposure)  

**WHERE ASSUMPTIONS ARE AGGRESSIVE**  
• Sustained ~35% EBITDA margins  
• Smooth execution of all capacity ramps  
• Strong steel spreads without major downcycle  
• High ROE despite heavy capex  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Not a pure mining play → emerging integrated steel story  
• Cost leadership + integration = structural advantage  
• Thriveni adds hidden value & cash flow stability  

**WHAT TO TRACK (CRITICAL)**  
• Ore ramp-up: 20 → 26 MTPA execution  
• Pellet + steel spreads (EBITDA/t)  
• Wire rod commissioning timeline  
• Net debt vs EBITDA discipline  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Conviction but High Risk) – Strong structural story, but execution & cycle sensitivity remain key

Buying the Future, Not Nostalgia

​The attached article serves as a sobering "reality check" for investors who equate a company's past glory with its future returns. While giants like Asian Paints, HUL, and TCS remain fundamentally strong businesses, the data from 2019 to 2026 highlights a critical lesson: valuation acts as gravity. When quality stocks are bid up to "priced-for-perfection" levels—sometimes exceeding 70x or 80x earnings—even solid profit growth can result in "lost decades" or paltry returns as multiples inevitably compress.

​The shift from the 2009–2019 "golden era" to the current slowdown proves that a great company can be a poor investment if the entry price ignores a margin of safety. As interest rates normalized and liquidity tightened, the market stopped paying for "nostalgia" and began demanding tangible earnings acceleration to justify high premiums. For the modern investor, the takeaway is clear: dominance in the market does not guarantee dominance in your portfolio if you overpay for the privilege of owning it.
